skill.name: python-pytest-patterns
skill.description: Guides Python developers through practical pytest patterns, including fixtures, parametrization, mocking, async tests, coverage, integration tests, and test architecture.
skill.summary: Apply proven pytest patterns for maintainable Python test suites.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most are false positives from Markdown code fences, pytest marker names, and placeholder test data. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because it includes executable Bash helpers that run pytest commands, write test files, and may install pytest-watch when watch mode is selected.

medium_findings.title: Executable Test Runner Accepts User Arguments
medium_findings.description: The run-tests helper executes pytest and appends unmatched command-line arguments to the pytest invocation. This is useful for a test helper, but users should review arguments before running it in sensitive repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly executes pytest with caller-provided arguments. The behavior is legitimate for a pytest helper, but it is real command execution rather than documentation only.

medium_findings.title: Helper Script Can Install a Package
medium_findings.description: The watch-mode branch checks for pytest-watch and runs pip install pytest-watch if it is missing. This creates a supply-chain and environment-modification concern if users run the script without reviewing it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The package installation command is explicit and reachable through the --watch option. It installs a named testing package, so intent appears legitimate but still changes the environment.

medium_findings.title: Generator Script Writes Project Test Configuration
medium_findings.description: The conftest generator writes tests/conftest.py and appends optional fixture blocks. It prompts before overwriting an existing file, but still performs filesystem writes in the caller project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script performs direct writes to tests/conftest.py. It includes an overwrite prompt, reducing severity, and the generated content is pytest boilerplate.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command findings point to Markdown fences, inline pytest commands, or setup examples. These are documentation snippets, not commands executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown examples and tables. No evidence found that these snippets run automatically when the skill is loaded.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Environment Values Are Test Data
low_findings.description: API_KEY, DATABASE_URL, and os.environ examples are test fixtures or mocking examples using dummy values. No evidence found that the skill reads real secrets or sends them elsewhere.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are static examples such as test-key and in-memory SQLite. They are used to demonstrate test isolation, not credential collection.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Misclassified Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts correspond to pytest marker text, Markdown headings, or table entries, not MD5, SHA1, or cryptographic code. No evidence found of weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found pytest labels and documentation content. There is no cryptographic operation at those locations.

low_findings.title: Documentation Includes Live Network Test Example
low_findings.description: One async testing example calls https://httpbin.org/json through an aiohttp test client. This is a documentation example and does not exfiltrate data, but users should avoid live external services in routine tests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded URL is real and external, but the example fetches public JSON and does not include credentials. The risk is reliability and unintended network access, not confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Autouse Fixture Clears Environment Variables
low_findings.description: An advanced fixture example clears os.environ before and after tests. This is a legitimate isolation pattern, but copying it without care can disrupt tests that need inherited environment variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment mutation is explicit in documentation. It is not malicious, but it can surprise users if used broadly.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Script Executes Pytest With Dynamic Arguments
dangerous_patterns.description: The test runner builds PYTEST_ARGS from recognized options and any unmatched user arguments, then invokes pytest with those values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic pytest invocation is real. It is expected for this helper, but users should treat shell helpers as executable code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Script Performs On-Demand Package Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: Watch mode installs pytest-watch when ptw is not present. This modifies the active Python environment and depends on package index resolution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pip install command is explicit and reachable. It installs a named package for a legitimate feature, so confidence is medium-high rather than very high.

content.value_statement: Python teams often need consistent pytest patterns across fixtures, mocks, async tests, and coverage. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical references and templates for maintainable test suites.
actual_capabilities: Explains pytest fixtures, fixture scopes, parametrization, markers, and exception assertions.
Provides reference patterns for mocking with unittest.mock, patch, MagicMock, and pytest-mock.
Covers async testing with pytest-asyncio, async fixtures, cancellation, and timeout examples.
Documents coverage workflows with pytest-cov, branch coverage, CI reports, and mutation testing references.
Includes templates for pytest.ini and conftest.py plus optional Bash helpers for test execution and fixture generation.
Shows integration, property-based, and test architecture patterns for larger Python projects.
limitations: The examples are generic and require adaptation to each project framework and dependency layout.
The Bash helpers should be reviewed before execution because they run commands and write files.
Some examples require optional packages such as pytest-asyncio, pytest-mock, pytest-cov, Hypothesis, or testcontainers.
The skill does not validate that generated tests match application behavior or business requirements.

use_cases.title: Standardize New Test Suites
use_cases.description: Create a consistent pytest layout with shared fixtures, markers, and configuration templates.
use_cases.target_user: Backend engineers starting a Python service

use_cases.title: Improve Existing Test Coverage
use_cases.description: Add parametrized tests, coverage reporting, and focused test architecture guidance to an existing codebase.

use_cases.title: Handle Advanced Testing Needs
use_cases.description: Apply async, integration, property-based, and mocking patterns for complex Python behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Pytest Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for simple unit tests.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-pytest-patterns skill to write basic pytest tests for this module. Include clear names, simple assertions, and pytest.raises where needed.

prompt_templates.title: Design Shared Fixtures
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-pytest-patterns skill to design fixtures and a conftest.py layout for this project. Explain fixture scopes and cleanup choices.

prompt_templates.title: Add Mocking and Coverage
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for isolated tests and quality gates.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-pytest-patterns skill to add mocking patterns and pytest-cov coverage guidance for this code. Avoid real network calls in unit tests.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Advanced Test Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for full test strategy.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-pytest-patterns skill to propose a test architecture for unit, integration, async, and property-based tests. Include risks and CI recommendations.

output_examples.input: Ask for pytest tests for a small Python utility module.
output_examples.output: A concise test plan with named test cases, fixture suggestions, expected assertions, and edge cases to cover.

output_examples.input: Ask for a testing strategy for a FastAPI service with a database.
output_examples.output: A layered testing approach covering unit tests, dependency overrides, transaction rollback fixtures, API client tests, and coverage reporting.

output_examples.input: Ask how to improve flaky async tests.
output_examples.output: Guidance on pytest-asyncio setup, timeout checks, cancellation tests, fixture cleanup, and avoiding live external services.

best_practices: Review shell scripts before running them, especially in repositories with sensitive configuration.
Keep unit tests isolated by mocking network, filesystem, and external service boundaries.
Use fixtures with clear scope and explicit cleanup to prevent hidden test coupling.
anti_patterns: Do not copy broad environment-clearing fixtures into a project without checking required variables.
Do not rely on live external URLs for routine unit or CI tests.
Do not treat coverage percentage as a substitute for meaningful assertions and edge-case tests.

faq.question: Does this skill run tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, templates, and optional scripts. Users choose whether to run any script.

faq.question: Can it help with pytest fixtures?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers fixture scopes, factories, autouse fixtures, finalization, and conftest.py organization.

faq.question: Does it support async Python tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes pytest-asyncio setup, async fixtures, timeout tests, and cancellation examples.

faq.question: Can it help with coverage reporting?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents pytest-cov commands, branch coverage, CI XML reports, and coverage thresholds.

faq.question: Are there security concerns?
faq.answer: The main concerns are optional Bash scripts that execute commands, write files, and may install pytest-watch.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
